market_context: The period opened with semiconductor stocks lifting the S&P 500 +0.19% on Jan 2 (first trading day of 2026), while Tesla's Q4 delivery miss (-16% YoY, 418k vs 423k expected) and ASML's double upgrade to Buy catalyzed immediate divergence in tech. CES 2026 (Jan 6-9) dominated the week: Nvidia's Vera Rubin AI platform announcement kept NVDA consolidating ~$185-189 while AMD rallied on Lisa Su's Ryzen AI keynote then faded. Small caps outperformed (IWM +1.1% Jan 2, reaching record highs by Jan 9) on Trump policy tailwinds including furniture tariff relief. Dec NFP (50k vs 73k expected, Jan 9) missed badly but unemployment fell to 4.4%; stocks rose as 'Goldilocks' soft-landing narrative held. Week of Jan 12-15 brought Jan 13 triple catalyst: JPM earnings beat but sold off -4.2% on IB fee miss and Trump's 10% credit card rate cap proposal, while Dec CPI came in cooler (core +2.6% YoY, lowest since 2021). Jan 15 saw GS report record IB revenues and 25% YoY fee growth; stock gapped up +4.6% on earnings day but the broader financial sector faced headwinds from the Powell DOJ investigation and credit card rate cap overhang. COIN and CEG were the regime's clearest shorts: COIN peaked early Jan on BTC euphoria then collapsed as risk appetite faded, while CEG's nuclear power premium eroded sharply as the AI data center power narrative was challenged by lower gas prices.

trades.thesis: Tesla reported Q4 2025 deliveries of 418,227 units, -16% YoY vs 495,570 in Q4 2024, and the second consecutive annual delivery decline. Stock priced at ~$438 embedded optimistic FSD/robotaxi optionality not yet validated. Post-delivery-miss sentiment fade historically precedes 2-4 week drift lower as growth deccel becomes consensus. 'Better than feared' analyst framing (Wedbush) historically marks near-term top.
trades.catalyst: Q4 2025 delivery miss released Jan 2 pre-market; annual decline narrative dominates next earnings cycle; no near-term FSD catalyst before Q4 earnings call late Jan
trades.lesson: Delivery miss shorting worked but modest (+3.7%) — TSLA bounced back to $445-449 in the Jan 9-12 window before fading; should have sized up more given clean catalyst

trades.thesis: Aletheia Capital double-upgrade from Sell to Buy with target $1,500, followed by Citi ($1,200), Cantor Fitzgerald ($1,300) and BofA ($1,331) price target hikes — all citing AI-driven EUV demand acceleration. ASML had been underowned post-China export ban fears; upgrades re-rate the stock toward 2027 DRAM market share inflection. The 8.8% gap-up on Jan 2 on heavy volume signals institutional re-accumulation, not a fade.
trades.catalyst: Multiple analyst double-upgrades on Jan 2 citing AI infrastructure capex cycle; TSMC DRAM demand ramp visible in lead orders; CES week AI infrastructure announcements reinforce thesis
trades.lesson: Best process trade of the period — multi-analyst re-rating with clean volume signal and AI infrastructure tailwind. The $1,436 exit exceeded the $1,350 target; holding through the period captured full institutional re-rating. The no-cheat framework would have exited at target; actual price ran further.

trades.thesis: Small caps +1.1% on Day 1 of 2026 — outperforming mega-cap tech which was flat/down. Trump's New Year's Eve postponement of furniture/cabinet tariffs signals domestic consumer relief. Russell 2000 historically front-runs rate sensitivity; softer jobs data ahead would reinforce small-cap rotation. Risk-reward compelling versus large-cap crowded longs.
trades.catalyst: Trump furniture tariff delay relief Jan 1; Jan 2 small-cap outperformance signal; Dec NFP miss Jan 9 (50k vs 73k) reinforces rate-cut-adjacent narrative; IWM hit all-time highs during the period
trades.lesson: Small cap rotation thesis played well; IWM reached all-time high ~$265 by Jan 14-15 before pulling back slightly. Entry on Day 1 momentum signal was clean.

trades.thesis: Coinbase trading at $254 embeds peak BTC euphoria from late 2025 crypto cycle. Crypto sentiment cycles are fast — once BTC consolidates, exchange volumes drop sharply and COIN P&L collapses. The Trump administration's crypto-friendly signals were already priced in by inauguration. Regulatory clarity narrative is 'buy the rumor sell the news.' Growth decel SaaS/exchange shorts are highest conviction when trading at 3-4x revenue.
trades.catalyst: BTC consolidation pattern visible Jan 5; post-inauguration 'sell the news' risk for crypto policy; volume data shows spot BTC ETF inflows decelerating; no new crypto catalyst until next BTC halving cycle
trades.lesson: Highest conviction short of the period and largest winner (+29.5%). Crypto exchange volumes collapsed with BTC as risk appetite faded across the period. The $179 exit hit before the Feb 5 low of $146 — left some on the table but a clean 30% short in 20 trading days is exceptional. Confirms: growth decel high-valuation crypto exchange shorts are among the highest-probability plays post-cycle peak.

trades.thesis: AMD CEO Lisa Su's CES keynote announced new Ryzen AI processors — the company is executing on AI PC cycle and gaining share vs Intel in data center GPU adjacency. AMD historically runs into CES on Lisa Su keynote momentum; at $210 (post-pullback from $223 Jan 2) the risk-reward is asymmetric heading into CES week.
trades.catalyst: Lisa Su CES keynote Jan 6; Ryzen AI PC processor announcements; competition with Intel's Core Ultra Series 3; AI PC platform cycle; NVIDIA's Vera Rubin announcement raises the floor for entire semiconductor sector
trades.lesson: CES 'buy the rumor sell the news' trap — AMD ran from $204 to $231 post-CES (Jan 15-20) then collapsed to $192 by Feb 5 as the AI PC thesis met hard demand data. The stock actually would have been a WIN if held to Jan 20-23 (+$249), then a LOSS. The 20-trading-day mechanical exit caught the draw-down. Lesson: semi longs post-CES need a fundamental earnings catalyst to sustain; product announces alone fade fast.

trades.thesis: Goldman Sachs heading into Q4 2025 earnings with visible M&A pipeline recovery. Investment banking fee cycle has been recovering since H2 2025 as deal-making resumed under rate stability. At $929, GS trades at ~10x forward P/E — cheap vs historical 12-14x for a bull market IB cycle. Jan 15 earnings are the near-term catalyst with high probability of beat given the M&A advisory #1 ranking for 2025.
trades.catalyst: Q4 2025 earnings Jan 15 with M&A advisory momentum; 25% YoY IB fee growth visible; Fed rate stability supports deal financing; Trump deregulation tailwinds for financial M&A
trades.lesson: The trade was correct on fundamentals — GS did spike to $970 on earnings day (Jan 15) — but the 20-day mechanical exit (Feb 6: $923) captured the post-earnings give-back. A nimble trader would have exited Jan 15-16 for a clean +4.4%. Mechanical 20-day holding is suboptimal for pre-earnings catalyst plays: earnings catalysts should use event-driven (sell into the number) rather than time-based exits.

trades.thesis: Dec NFP missed badly (50k vs 73k expected). While unemployment ticked down to 4.4%, the hiring deceleration trend (ISM manufacturing contracted 10th consecutive month) signals labor market cooling. Bonds should benefit from 'soft landing' narrative. TLT at $86.64 offers asymmetric upside if subsequent CPI data also comes in soft. The 10-year yield falling bias should support long-duration bonds.
trades.catalyst: Dec NFP miss Jan 9 (50k vs 73k); ISM manufacturing -10th consecutive contraction; CPI Jan 13 shows core at lowest since 2021; rate cut probability rising; Treasury yields declining
trades.lesson: Bond thesis was directionally correct (TLT did rise to $88.74 by Feb 13) but the 20-day exit on Feb 9 ($86.56) returned flat. The CPI-driven bond rally came after the exit window. Timing mismatch between fundamental catalyst and mechanical exit period. TLT is a slow-moving instrument — 20-day windows are often too short for rate thesis trades to fully develop.

trades.thesis: JPMorgan beat on EPS ($5.23 vs $4.86 expected) but IB fees disappointed — both underwriting and M&A advisory revenue dropped. Classic 'sell the news' pattern on a crowded long. Additionally Trump proposed a 1-year cap on credit card interest rates at 10% (vs current ~22%), directly attacking JPM's consumer credit business model. Stock already fell -4.2% on the earnings day itself, signaling distribution. Short into the credit card rate cap overhang.
trades.catalyst: JPM earnings sell-off Jan 13 (-4.2% on earnings day); Trump 10% credit card rate cap proposal effective Jan 20; Powell DOJ investigation (criminal) reduces Fed coordination visibility; bank sector rotation out
trades.lesson: SHORT JPM entered after the -4.2% earnings day decline already happened — the big move was already in. The stock bounced between $296-315 over the next 20 days, returning essentially flat at exit. Should have entered the SHORT on Jan 12 (pre-earnings, capturing the sell-the-news), not Jan 13 after the move. Lesson: post-event short entries after a large single-day move often mean you're chasing.

trades.thesis: Goldman Sachs Q4 2025 blowout: EPS $14.01 vs est ~$12.50, record $41.5B Global Banking & Markets revenue (+18% YoY), IB fees +25% YoY, dividend raised 50% to $4.50/quarter. Stock gapped from $927 to $970 on earnings. Thesis: sustained multiple re-rating as IB cycle accelerates and Trump deregulation supports M&A pipeline into 2026. Entry post-earnings-gap into close for continuation.
trades.catalyst: GS Q4 2025 beats across all metrics Jan 15; record IB revenues; 50% dividend hike; M&A pipeline acceleration; Trump deregulation M&A tailwinds for 2026
trades.lesson: Bought the gap-up into close on earnings day — classic mistake. GS immediately gave back the earnings premium as the credit card rate cap (banks broadly hurt) and Trump tariff escalation weighed on financials sector. The stock fell from $970 to $900 (-7.2%) over 20 days. Entry into an earnings gap-up on a financial during a period of regulatory/political headwinds was poor risk management. Should have waited for a post-earnings consolidation base.

trades.thesis: Constellation Energy (CEG) at $365 represents peak nuclear power premium driven by AI data center power narrative. The stock had rallied 300%+ on the Microsoft/Three Mile Island deal. At $365, the valuation embeds heroic assumptions about new nuclear capacity and AI demand growth that are structurally constrained by NRC approval timelines (7-10 years). No new nuclear catalyst is imminent; lower natural gas prices (which CEG competes against) are a valuation headwind. The AI data center power narrative had become consensus = priced in per F15 framework.
trades.catalyst: Nuclear power narrative overbought vs physical constraint reality; natural gas prices declining; no new reactor deals visible near-term; consensus 'AI power' narrative = priced in; ASML/semiconductor upgrades drawing capital away from nuclear power plays
trades.lesson: Highest conviction fundamental short: >20 trading day decline of 26% confirms the thesis that nuclear power premium was unsustainable without near-term catalysts. CEG fell from $365 to $270 — and continued declining further. The supply-side constraint framework (F2) correctly identified that NRC approval timelines make the bull thesis structurally implausible at those valuations. Physical AI power constraints are real but 10-year nuclear capacity can't solve 2026 AI demand.

period_summary.regime_notes: Jan 2-15 2026 regime: (1) AI infrastructure re-rating underway — semiconductor equipment (ASML) benefiting most, GPU makers consolidating. (2) Crypto/speculative assets peaked — COIN, MARA, MSTR all entered structural downtrends as BTC consolidated. (3) Small caps outperformed mega-cap in early Jan on tariff relief and domestic economic optimism. (4) Financial sector: strong fundamentals (GS/JPM beat) but political headwinds (credit card rate cap, Powell DOJ) capped upside. (5) Nuclear power premium (CEG) deflated sharply — the AI data center power thesis was consensus = priced in = short. (6) CES 2026 was a 'buy the rumor sell the news' event for most semis except ASML (multi-analyst re-rating was different in kind from product hype). (7) Dec CPI (core 2.6%) + NFP miss (50k) = Goldilocks alive but rate cut path still 'pause' — bonds flat/modestly positive. (8) The biggest regime error was buying AMD into CES: product announcements ≠ fundamental earnings catalyst for semiconductor longs.
